Those Magnificent Men in their Flying Machines

[Indian Army](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/indian-army/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Those Magnificent Men in their Flying Machines

### 1965 War

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Sep 06, 2023

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

Share

The 1965 Indo-Pak war marked a dramatic shift for the Indian Air Force from logistical support to full-spectrum aerial combat, and it’s no exaggeration to say that the skies over Punjab and Kashmir became a proving ground for both air forces.

During the 1947-48 War, the IAF was largely a logistical lifeline—airlifting troops, supplies, and evacuating casualties. There was minimal air-to-air combat, with only one notable interception of a Pakistani transport aircraft.

It was the 1965 War, that saw it evolve as a fighting unit,flew approximately **3,937 sorties**, while the PAF flew around **2,364**. It was the first time **aerial dogfights** occurred in an Indo-Pak war. The IAF’s **Folland Gnats** earned the nickname “Sabre Slayers” for their success against the PAF’s F-86 Sabres.The war saw **close air support**, **interdiction missions**, and **counter-air operations**.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bLxH!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fed91af27-20a1-44e7-89f8-640bc1cd62cf_473x286.jpeg)

![apt](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bLxH!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fed91af27-20a1-44e7-89f8-640bc1cd62cf_473x286.jpeg "apt")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Air Chief Marshal Arjan Singh, then just 46, led the Indian Air Force with a calm decisiveness that earned him the Padma Vibhushan. His strategic clarity helped the IAF transition from a support wing to a combat-ready force capable of offensive and defensive operations.

While India claimed to have downed 73 Pakistani aircraft and lost 35, and Pakistan claimed 104 Indian kills with only 19 losses, neutral estimates suggest **India lost 60–75 aircraft**, while **Pakistan lost around 20**. These figures reflect the brutal intensity of the conflict, not just in numbers but in morale and myth-making. The 1965 war saw some of the **most intense dogfights** since World War II in South Asia. The skies over Punjab and Kashmir echoed with the roar of Gnats, Mystères,

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!OST7!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6feba430-f4ad-436c-8800-254cdb833832_318x159.jpeg)

![jl](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!OST7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6feba430-f4ad-436c-8800-254cdb833832_318x159.jpeg "jl")

**September 1**

Led by Wing Commander S.K. “Marshal” Dhar, No. 45 Squadron had just relocated from Pune to Pathankot. Tasked with immediate retaliation, they launched three waves of **four Vampire Mk 52s each**, targeting Pakistani armor and troop concentrations in Chhamb.

The Vampires strafed enemy positions with rockets and cannon fire, reportedly causing **significant disruption** to Pakistan’s advancing forces. It was a daring low-level assault, executed with resolve but limited by the aircraft’s aging design.

The counter came swiftly. Two **PAF F-86 Sabres**, piloted by **Sqn Ldr Sarfaraz Rafiqui** and **Flt Lt Imtiaz Bhatti**, intercepted the Vampires. In the ensuing clash, **four Vampires were shot down**, one by ground fire and three by Sabres.

The losses exposed the vulnerability of the Vampire fleet. The IAF withdrew **130 Vampires** and **50 Ouragons** from frontline service, marking a doctrinal shift toward more agile and modern aircraft like the **Folland Gnats** and **Mystère IVs**.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!dLjn!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F14b01ff2-6016-4a1f-9436-1c96e6cdf146_345x194.jpeg)

![kb1](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!dLjn!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F14b01ff2-6016-4a1f-9436-1c96e6cdf146_345x194.jpeg "kb1")

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mWur!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fab5ed16f-7dd2-4583-a137-317519aaaada_287x194.jpeg)

![sc](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mWur!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fab5ed16f-7dd2-4583-a137-317519aaaada_287x194.jpeg "sc")

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WyNi!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fdbda004b-7584-4c5e-ba54-72278e422c3d_281x180.jpeg)

![mahanadi](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WyNi!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fdbda004b-7584-4c5e-ba54-72278e422c3d_281x180.jpeg "mahanadi")

On Septemebr 3, the Pakistan Air Force launched a **large-scale Sabre attack**, confident in their superior aircraft and Sidewinder missile capability. The Indian Air Force responded with a **strategic masterstroke**, deploying **slow-flying Mystère IVs** as bait, knowing the Sabres would be drawn to easy prey.

As expected, the Sabres engaged. But lurking above and behind were the **Folland Gnats**, small, nimble, and deadly. The Gnats pounced, forcing two Sabres to retreat. One, piloted by **Flt Lt Yusuf Ali Khan**, stayed to fight, attempting to lock onto the Gnats.

In a moment of aerial choreography, a **cloud of Gnats surrounded Yusuf’s Sabre**, engaging in a tight dogfight. Just as the Gnats seemed poised to finish the engagement, a **Lockheed F-104 Starfighter**, Pakistan’s supersonic ace, entered the fray. Its speed and firepower forced the Gnats to disengage, preserving their tactical advantage but conceding the kill.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wMND!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc7ea4afa-25ea-4f18-af34-6fc58b96d126_200x240.jpeg)

![apt](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wMND!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc7ea4afa-25ea-4f18-af34-6fc58b96d126_200x240.jpeg "apt")

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!InEx!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F60eaed18-9d92-40dd-b684-9219b43295d5_397x283.jpeg)

![jl](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!InEx!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F60eaed18-9d92-40dd-b684-9219b43295d5_397x283.jpeg "jl")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The hero of the day was Squadron Leader Trevor Keelor, who shot down one of the Sabres, and was later awarded the Vir Chakra. Born in Lucknow, educated at *La Martinière College*, he joined the IAF in 1953 and rose to the rank of Wing Commander before retiring in 1978.

On **September 3, 1965**, flying a Folland Gnat, he engaged a formation of PAF Sabres in a daring dogfight. Despite being outnumbered and facing the technologically superior **F-104 Starfighter**, he chased and shot down a Sabre, **the first confirmed air-to-air kill by the IAF** in independent India.

His elder brother Denzil Keeler, also a La Martinière alumnus, joined the IAF in 1954. On **September 19, 1965**, he led a Gnat formation escorting Mystère IVs when they were ambushed by Sabres. Under his command, one Sabre was shot down, and he himself crippled another. He too received the **Vir Chakra**, making them the only siblings to be awarded for identical feats in the same war.

Another **Squadron Leader Brij Pal Singh Sikand,** mistakenly landed at **Pasrur on September 3**, an abandoned Pakistani airstrip, believing it to be Indian territory.He was taken **Prisoner of War**, and his aircraft was later displayed as a war trophy at the PAF Museum in Karachi.

**Flight Lieutenant Hakimullah Khan Durrani,**flying a **Lockheed F-104 Starfighter**, he intercepted Sikand’s Gnat and forced it down. He was later honored by the **Pakistan Government** for his role in the engagement. He went on to become **Air Chief Marshal** and served as Pakistan’s Chief of Air Staff from 1988 to 1991.

As the Indian Army crossed the international border on September 6 and advanced toward Lahore to relieve pressure in Kashmir, the Pakistan Air Force launched a **three-pronged aerial counterstrike** targeting **Pathankot, Adampur, and Halwara**.

The most succesful raid was at Pathankot, where PAF’s No. 19 Squadron, flying **F-86 Sabres**, destroyed **10 IAF aircraft on the ground**, including **MiG-21s, Mystère IVs, Hunters, and Vampires. The Adampur mission led by M.M. Alam**, was **aborted mid-flight**, possibly due to radar detection or tactical reassessment.

The raid at Halwara led by **qn Ldr Sarfaraz Rafiqui**, was met with fierce resistance. **IAF Hawker Hunters** intercepted the attackers, shooting down **three Sabres**, including Rafiqui himself. Rafiqui had earlier shot down **two Vampires on September 1**, earning acclaim as a rising ace. His death was a major blow to PAF morale.

On the night of September 7, Pakistan parachuted 135 SSG in three waves using **C-130 Hercules aircraft** commands with the objective of crippling **Pathankot, Adampur, Halwar** through sabotage and disruption.

Each group had 45 elite troops, dropped near their respective targets. India lacked night-capable interceptors, making the drop feasible. However the operation was a **complete disaster, with** only **10 commandos returned**; the rest were **captured as POWs.**

Local villagers, police, and base personnel quickly mobilized to neutralize the threat. The plan, though bold, suffered from poor coordination and intelligence.

IAF on the same date carried out 33 air strikes on heavily fortified **Sargodha Airbase**, using **Mystère IVs** and **Hawker Hunters,** focusing on the **runways, hangars, and parked aircraft.**

Despite losing **2 Mystères** and **3 Hunters, IAF** managed to **inflict damage** and demonstrate reach, as the raids marked a shift from reactive defence to **offensive air doctrine.**

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!HlbZ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2bf61556-4fd8-44ce-b102-d36740ec7ecd_190x240.jpeg)

![kb](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!HlbZ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2bf61556-4fd8-44ce-b102-d36740ec7ecd_190x240.jpeg "kb")

The hero of the day was Ajjamada Devaiah, of the No 1 Tigers Squadron, who shot down a Pakistani Lockheed Starfighter during an intense dogfight. Born in **Coorg** to Dr. Bopayya, Devayya joined the Indian Air Force in **1954**, and was posted to the elite **No. 1 “Tigers” Squadron**, flying the **Mystère IVA Fighter-Bomber**.

On **September 7, 1965**, Devayya was on **standby** for a strike mission targeting **Sargodha**, Pakistan’s most fortified airbase. When one pilot dropped out, he stepped in unflinching, unheralded.

Intercepted by a **PAF F-104 Starfighter** piloted by **Flt Lt Amjad Hussain**, Devayya’s slower Mystère was damaged. Yet, in a stunning counterattack, he **struck the Starfighter**, forcing Hussain to eject.

Devayya’s aircraft, low on fuel and battered, **crash-landed in Pakistani territory**. His body was later found by villagers near Sargodha.

For years, his fate remained unknown. It was only through **Pakistani accounts** and British author **John Fricker’s commissioned history** that the IAF learned of his heroism. In **1988**, **23 years later**, Devayya was awarded the **Maha Vir Chakra**—India’s second-highest wartime gallantry award. He remains the **only IAF officer to receive it posthumously**.

Devayya is revered alongside legends like **Field Marshal Cariappa** and **General Thimayya**. In **Madikeri**, the **private bus stand circle** bears his name—a civic homage to a silent sentinel of the skies.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!obU5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbaa8154d-8304-4c94-8c06-bbb27fd08b29_197x256.jpeg)

![kb1](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!obU5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbaa8154d-8304-4c94-8c06-bbb27fd08b29_197x256.jpeg "kb1")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!abgw!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffa527666-dd01-4bf5-ba5b-ab91cb300fc0_289x224.jpeg)

![sc](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!abgw!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffa527666-dd01-4bf5-ba5b-ab91cb300fc0_289x224.jpeg "sc")

The other hero of the day was Flt Lt Alfred T Cooke, who engaged 3 PAF Sabres in a straight dogfight, flying a Hawker Hunter. A proud alumnus of *La Martinière College, Lucknow*, Cooke joined the IAF and was posted to **No. 14 Squadron**, flying the **Hawker Hunter**, a sleek and powerful jet known for its agility and firepower.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!P9L0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc9268d0a-114a-4aa8-bc16-dc45f82853c8_259x194.jpeg)

![mahanadi](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!P9L0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc9268d0a-114a-4aa8-bc16-dc45f82853c8_259x194.jpeg "mahanadi")

On **September 7, 1965**, the **PAF launched multiple raids** on the Kalaikunda airbase, situated near the **Kharagpur–Tatanagar railway line**. The first wave damaged **four Vampires and two Canberras**. A second wave of **four Sabres**, flying low and fast, attempted to finish the job.

Leading a **two-aircraft patrol** with Flying Officer S.C. Mamgain, Cooke was vectored toward the incoming Sabres. He **engaged three Sabres alone**, diving into the dogfight with fearless precision. He **shot down one Sabre mid-air**, damaged another so severely it crashed across the border, and **chased the third** despite running out of ammunition, forcing it to retreat.

Cooke was awarded the **Vir Chakra** for his audacious bravery. His feat is considered one of the **greatest 1-vs-3 air combats** in IAF history.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Hmxn!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe3328537-b4b9-4391-86fd-bab4a4eae151_244x165.jpeg)

![apt](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Hmxn!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe3328537-b4b9-4391-86fd-bab4a4eae151_244x165.jpeg "apt")

The Folland Gnat nicknamed the Sabre Slayer was the star of the show, credited with shooting down at least 7 Pakistani Sabres.

Credited with **at least 7 Sabre kills** during the war. Its small size, tight turning radius, and rapid climb rate made it a nightmare for Sabre pilots. Pakistani pilots reportedly struggled to spot the Gnat until it was too late—earning it the reputation of a “mosquito with a sting.”

**No. 9 and No. 23 Squadrons** of the IAF were the principal Gnat operators during the war. These squadrons flew **200+ missions**, often in high-risk interception and air superiority roles.

The IAF used Gnats in **ambush tactics**, hiding them behind slower Mystère IVs to lure Sabres into attack range. Once engaged, the Gnats would swarm in, outmaneuvering and outgunning the Sabres in close combat.

The Gnat’s performance led to its evolution into the **HAL Ajeet**, a more advanced Indian variant. It remains one of the few aircraft in history to earn a nickname based on its kill record against a superior adversary.

While the IAF did manage to hold it’s own, against the much superior PAF aircraft, it did learn many lessons, and changed it’s tactics. The 1965 war exposed India’s limited **ground-based radar network**, especially in low-level detection. This left airbases vulnerable to surprise raids like those on Pathankot and Kalaikunda.

IAF fighters, including the Gnat and Hunter, relied on **guns and maneuvering**, while PAF Sabres and Starfighters used **Sidewinder missiles**, giving them a standoff advantage. ndia acquired the **SNR-75 “Fan Song E” radar**, a Soviet fire-control system designed to guide **SA-2 Guideline surface-to-air missiles**.

It was capable of tracking **multiple targets simultaneously,** linked to **SAM batteries** for high-altitude interception and operated in **E/F and G bands**, with a range of up to **145 km.**

India deployed **ZSU-23-4 Shilka systems**, **Bofors guns**, and other Soviet-origin AA platforms to protect key installations. By 1971, IAF aircraft like the **MiG-21** were equipped with **K-13 (AA-2 Atoll)** missiles, leveling the playing field in dogfights

Though India was officially non-aligned, the embargo accelerated its tilt toward the **Soviet Union**, which had already supported India during the 1962 Sino-Indian War. India diversified its military imports, with MiG-21s, SA-2 SAMs, radar systems, T-54 tanks from Soviet Union, Mystère IVs, Ouragans, and AMX-13 tanks from France and Hawker Hunters, Canberras, and Centurion tanks from Britain.

The embargo forced India to invest in **indigenous maintenance**, **tactical innovation**, and **radar-missile integration**, laying the groundwork for its 1971 air dominance.

Pakistan on the other hand had built its air force around **American platforms**—F-86 Sabres, F-104 Starfighters, and Patton tanks—supplied under **SEATO and CENTO** alliances. The embargo **cut off spare parts and upgrades**, grounding many aircraft. Pakistan had to **source second-hand Sabres from Iran**, which were outdated. It acquired **Mirage IIIs from France**, but integration was slow and costly.

The lack of diversified suppliers and indigenous capability left Pakistan vulnerable, especially in the Eastern theater during the 1971 war.

When the 1965 war began, Pakistan held the technological edge—Sabres, Starfighters, and American support. But within **72 hours**, Arjan Singh recalibrated the IAF’s posture, restoring **operational balance** and launching **precision strikes** that disrupted Pakistani supply lines.

He ordered aircraft to **fly over Kashmir’s mountainous terrain**, avoiding Pakistani radar detection. He prioritized **interdiction missions**, targeting **railheads, fuel depots, and troop convoys**, crippling Pakistan’s war logistics. His leadership turned the IAF from a reactive force into a **strategic spearhead**.

Post-1965, Singh spearheaded the acquisition of **radar systems**, **SAM networks**, and **missile-equipped fighters**. He laid the groundwork for the **Air Force Academy (1967)** and **Armament Training Wing at Jamnagar**, institutionalizing excellence. Under his tenure, the IAF expanded to **36 squadrons** by 1971, with **over 650 combat aircraft**, giving India decisive air superiority.

Singh was the **first and only IAF officer** to be promoted to **Marshal of the Indian Air Force**, a five-star rank equivalent to Field Marshal. He flew **over 60 types of aircraft**, from biplanes to jets, and was awarded the **Distinguished Flying Cross** during WWII.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2
